## BOOTSTRAPPED META-LEARNING

## **Sebastian Flennerhag**

DeepMind flennerhag@google.com

**Yannick Schroecker Tom Zahavy** DeepMind DeepMind

**Hado van Hasselt David Silver Satinder Singh** DeepMind DeepMind DeepMind

## ABSTRACT

Meta-learning empowers artificial intelligence to increase its efficiency by learning how to learn. Unlocking this potential involves overcoming a challenging metaoptimisation problem. We propose an algorithm that tackles this problem by letting the meta-learner teach itself. The algorithm first bootstraps a _target_ from the metalearner, then optimises the meta-learner by minimising the _distance_ to that target under a chosen (pseudo-)metric. Focusing on meta-learning with gradients, we establish conditions that guarantee performance improvements and show that the metric can control meta-optimisation. Meanwhile, the bootstrapping mechanism can extend the effective meta-learning horizon without requiring backpropagation through all updates. We achieve a new state-of-the art for model-free agents on the Atari ALE benchmark and demonstrate that it yields both performance and efficiency gains in multi-task meta-learning. Finally, we explore how bootstrapping opens up new possibilities and find that it can meta-learn efficient exploration in an _ε_ -greedy _Q_ -learning agent—without backpropagating through the update rule.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

In a standard machine learning problem, a _learner_ or _agent_ learns a task by iteratively adjusting its parameters under a given update rule, such as Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD). Typically, the learner’s update rule must be tuned manually. In contrast, humans learn seamlessly by relying on previous experiences to inform their learning processes (Spelke & Kinzler, 2007).

For a (machine) learner to have the same capability, it must be able to learn its update rule (or such inductive biases). _Meta-learning_ is one approach that learns (parts of) an update rule by applying it for some number of steps and then evaluating the resulting performance (Schmidhuber, 1987; Hinton & Plaut, 1987; Bengio et al., 1991). For instance, a well-studied and often successful approach is to tune parameters of a gradient-based update, either online during training on a single task (Bengio, 2000; Maclaurin et al., 2015; Xu et al., 2018; Zahavy et al., 2020), or meta-learned over a distribution of tasks (Finn et al., 2017; Rusu et al., 2019; Flennerhag et al., 2020; Jerfel et al., 2019; Denevi et al., 2019). More generally, the update rule can be an arbitrary parameterised function (Hochreiter et al., 2001; Andrychowicz et al., 2016; Kirsch et al., 2019; Oh et al., 2020), or the function itself can be meta-learned jointly with its parameters (Alet et al., 2020; Real et al., 2020).

Meta-learning is challenging because to evaluate an update rule, it must first be applied. This often leads to high computational costs. As a result most works optimise performance after _K_ applications of the update rule and assume that this yields improved performance for the remainder of the learner’s lifetime (Bengio et al., 1991; Maclaurin et al., 2015; Metz et al., 2019). When this assumption fails, meta-learning suffers from a short-horizon bias (Wu et al., 2018; Metz et al., 2019). Similarly, optimizing the learner’s performance after _K_ updates can fail to account for the _process_ of learning, causing another form of myopia (Flennerhag et al., 2019; Stadie et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2016; Cao et al., 2019). Challenges in meta-optimisation have been observed to cause degraded lifetime performance (Lv et al., 2017; Wichrowska et al., 2017), collapsed exploration (Stadie et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2016), biased learner updates (Stadie et al., 2018; Zheng et al., 2018), and poor generalisation performance (Wu et al., 2018; Yin et al., 2020; Triantafillou et al., 2020).

We argue that defining the meta-learner’s objective directly in terms of the learner’s objective—i.e. the performance after _K_ update steps—creates two bottlenecks in meta-optimisation. The first bottleneck is curvature: the meta-objective is constrained to the same type of geometry as the learner; the second is myopia: the meta-objective is fundamentally limited to evaluating performance within the _K_ -step horizon, but ignores future learning dynamics. Our goal is to design an algorithm that removes these.

The algorithm relies on two main ideas. First, to mitigate myopia, we introduce the notion of bootstrapping a target from the meta-learner itself, a _meta-bootstrap_ , that infuses information about learning dynamics in the objective. Second, to control curvature, we formulate the meta-objective in terms of minimising distance (or divergence) to the bootstrapped target, thereby controlling the meta-loss landscape. In this way, the meta-learner learns from its future self. This leads to a bootstrapping effect where improvements beget further improvements. We present a detailed formulation in Section 3; on a high level, as in previous works, we first unroll the meta-learned update rule for _K_ steps to obtain the learner’s new parameters. Whereas standard meta-objectives optimise the update rule with respect to (w.r.t.) the learner’s performance under the new parameters, our proposed algorithm constructs the meta-objective in two steps:

1. It _bootstraps_ a _target_ from the learner’s new parameters. In this paper, we generate targets by continuing to update the learner’s parameters—either under the meta-learned update rule or another update rule—for some number of steps.

2. The learner’s new parameters—which are a function of the meta-learner’s parameters—and the target are projected onto a _matching space_ . A simple example is Euclidean parameter space. To control curvature, we may choose a different (pseudo-)metric space. For instance, a common choice under probabilistic models is the Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence.

The meta-learner is optimised by minimising distance to the bootstrapped target. We focus on gradient-based optimisation, but other optimisation routines are equally applicable. By optimising meta-parameters in a well-behaved space, we can drastically reduce ill-conditioning and other phenomena that disrupt meta-optimisation. In particular, this form of _Bootstrapped Meta-Gradient_ (BMG) enables us to infuse information about _future_ learning dynamics without increasing the number of update steps to backpropagate through. In effect, the meta-learner becomes its own teacher. We show that BMG can guarantee performance improvements (Theorem 1) and that this guarantee can be stronger than under standard meta-gradients (Corollary 1). Empirically, we find that BMG provides substantial performance improvements over standard meta-gradients in various settings. We obtain a new state-of-the-art result for model-free agents on Atari (Section 5.2) and improve upon MAML (Finn et al., 2017) in the few-shot setting (Section 6). Finally, we demonstrate how BMG enables new forms of meta-learning, exemplified by meta-learning _ε_ -greedy exploration (Section 5.1).

## 2 RELATED WORK

Bootstrapping as used here stems from temporal difference (TD) algorithms in reinforcement learning (RL) (Sutton, 1988). In these algorithms, an agent learns a value function by using its own future predictions as targets. Bootstrapping has recently been introduced in the self-supervised setting (Guo et al., 2020; Grill et al., 2020). In this paper, we introduce the idea of bootstrapping in the context of meta-learning, where a meta-learner learns about an update rule by generating future targets from it.

Our approach to target matching is related to methods in multi-task meta-learning (Flennerhag et al., 2019; Nichol et al., 2018) that meta-learn an initialisation for SGD by minimising the Euclidean distance to task-optimal parameters. BMG generalise this concept by allowing for arbitrary metaparameters, matching functions, and target bootstraps. It is further related the more general concept of self-referential meta-learning (Schmidhuber, 1987; 1993), where the meta-learned update rule is used to optimise its own meta-objective.

Target matching under KL divergences results in a form of distillation (Hinton et al., 2015), where an online network (student) is encouraged to match a target network (teacher). In a typical setup, the target is either a fixed (set of) expert(s) (Hinton et al., 2015; Rusu et al., 2015) or a moving aggregation of current experts (Teh et al., 2017; Grill et al., 2020), whereas BMG bootstraps a target by following an update rule. Finally, BMG is loosely inspired by trust-region methods that introduce a distance function to regularize gradient updates (Pascanu & Bengio, 2014; Schulman et al., 2015; Tomar et al., 2020; Hessel et al., 2021).

## 3 BOOTSTRAPPED META-GRADIENTS

We begin in the single-task setting and turn to multi-task meta-learning in Section 6. The learner’s problem is to minimize a stochastic objective _f_ ( **x** ) := E[ _ℓ_ ( **x** ; _**ζ**_ )] over a data distribution _p_ ( _**ζ**_ ), where _**ζ**_ denotes a source of data and **x** _∈X ⊂_ R _[n][x]_ denotes the learner’s parameters. In RL, _f_ is typically the (negative) expected value of a policy _π_ **x** ; in supervised learning, _f_ may be the expected negative loglikelihood under a probabilistic model _π_ **x** . We provide precise formulations in Sections 5 and 6.

The meta-learner’s problem is to learn an update rule _ϕ_ : _X × H × W →X_ that updates the learner’s parameters by **x**[(1)] = **x** + _ϕ_ ( **x** _,_ **h** _,_ **w** ) given **x** _∈X_ , a learning state **h** _∈H_ , and meta-parameters **w** _∈W ⊂_ R _[n][w]_ of the update rule. We make no assumptions on the update rule other than differentiability in **w** . As such, _ϕ_ can be a recurrent neural network (Hochreiter et al., 2001; Wang et al., 2016; Andrychowicz et al., 2016) or gradient descent (Bengio, 2000; Maclaurin et al., 2015; Finn et al., 2017). The learning state **h** contains any other data required to compute the update; in a black-box setting **h** contains an observation and the recurrent state of the network; for gradient-based updates, **h** contains the (estimated) gradient of _f_ at **x** along with any auxiliary information; for instance, SGD is given by **x**[(1)] = **x** _−α∇xf_ ( **x** ) with **h** = _∇xf_ ( **x** ) _,_ **w** = _α ∈_ R+.

The standard meta-gradient (MG) optimises meta-parameters **w** by taking _K_ steps under _ϕ_ and evaluating the resulting learner parameter vector under _f_ . With a slight abuse of notation, let **x**[(] _[K]_[)] ( **w** ) denote the learner’s parameters after _K_ applications of _ϕ_ starting from some ( **x** _,_ **h** _,_ **w** ), where ( **x** _,_ **h** ) evolve according to _ϕ_ and the underlying data distribution. The MG update is defined by

Extensions involve averaging the performance over all iterates **x**[(1)] _, . . . ,_ **x**[(] _[K]_[)] (Andrychowicz et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2016; Antoniou et al., 2019) or using validation data in the meta-objective (Bengio et al., 1991; Maclaurin et al., 2015; Finn et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2018). We observe two bottlenecks in the meta-objective in Eq. 1. First, the meta-objective is subject to the same curvature as the learner. Thus if _f_ is ill-conditioned, so will the meta-objective be. Second, the meta-objective is only able to evaluate the meta-learner on dynamics up to the _K_ th step, but ignores effects of future updates.

To tackle myopia, we introduce a _Target Bootstrap_ (TB) _ξ_ : _X �→X_ that maps the meta-learner’s output **x**[(] _[K]_[)] into a bootstrapped target ˜ **x** = _ξ_ ( **x**[(] _[K]_[)] ). We focus on TBs that unroll _ϕ_ a further _L −_ 1 steps ˜ before taking final gradient step on _f_ , with targets of the form **x** = **x**[(] _[K]_[+] _[L][−]_[1)] _−α∇f_ ( **x**[(] _[K]_[+] _[L][−]_[1)] ). This TB encourages the meta-learner to reach future states on its trajectory faster while nudging the trajectory in a descent direction. Crucially, regardless of the bootstrapping strategy, we do _not_ backpropagate through the target. Akin to temporal difference learning in RL (Sutton, 1988), the target is a fixed goal that the meta-learner should try to produce within the _K_ -step budget.

Finally, to improve the meta-optimisation landscape, we introduce a _matching function µ_ : _X × X →_ R+ that measures the (dis)similarity between the meta-learner’s output, **x**[(] _[K]_[)] ( **w** ), and the target, **x** ˜, in a matching space defined by _µ_ (see Figure 1). **Matching Function** Of primary concern to us are models that output a probabilistic distribution, _π_ **x** . A common pseudo-metric over a space of probability distributions is the Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence. For instance, Natural Gradients (Amari, 1998) point in the direction of steepest descent under the KL-divergence, often approximated through a KL-regularization term (Pascanu & Bengio, 2014). KL-divergences also arise naturally in RL algorithms (Kakade, 2001; Schulman et al., 2015; 2017; Abdolmaleki et al., 2018). Hence, a natural starting point is to consider KL-divergences between the target and the iterate, e.g. _µ_ (˜ **x** _,_ **x**[(] _[K]_[)] ) = KL ( _π_ **x** ˜ _∥ π_ **x** ( _K_ )). In actor-critic algorithms (Sutton et al., 1999), the policy defines only part of the agent—the value function defines the other. Thus, we also consider a composite matching function over both policy and value function.

**Target Bootstrap** We analyze conditions under which BMG guarantees performance improvements in Section 4 and find that the target should co-align with the gradient direction. Thus, in this paper we focus on gradient-based TBs and find that they perform well empirically. As with matching functions, this is a small subset of all possible choices; we leave the exploration of other choices for future work.

## 4 PERFORMANCE GUARANTEES

In this analysis, we restrict attention to the noise-less setting (true expectations). In this setting, we ask three questions: (1) what local performance guarantees are provided by MG? (2) What performance guarantees can BMG provide? (3) How do these guarantees relate to each other? To answer these questions, we analyse how the performance around _f_ ( **x**[(] _[K]_[)] ( **w** )) changes by updating **w** either under standard meta-gradients (Eq. 1) or bootstrapped meta-gradients (Eq. 2).

First, consider improvements under the MG update. In online optimisation, the MG update can achieve strong convergence guarantees if the problem is well-behaved (van Erven & Koolen, 2016), with similar guarantees in the multi-task setting (Balcan et al., 2019; Khodak et al., 2019; Denevi et al., 2019). **Discussion** Our analysis focuses on an arbitrary (albeit noiseless) objective _f_ and establishes that BMG can guarantee improved performance under a variety of TBs. We further show that BMG can yield larger local improvements than MG. To identify optimal TBs, further assumptions are required on _f_ and _µ_ , but given these Theorem 1 can serve as a starting point for more specialised analysis. Empirically, we find that taking _L_ steps on the meta-learned update with an final gradient step on the objective performs well. Theorem 1 exposes a trade-off for targets that are “far” away. Empirically, we observe clear benefits from bootstraps that unroll the meta-learner for several steps before taking a gradient step on _f_ ; exploring other forms of bootstraps is an exciting area for future research.

## 5 REINFORCEMENT LEARNING

We consider a typical reinforcement learning problem, modelled as an MDP _M_ = ( _S, A, P, R, γ_ ). Given an initial state **s** 0 _∈S_ , at each time step _t ∈_ N, the agent takes an action **a** _t ∼ π_ **x** ( **a** _|_ **s** _t_ ) from a _policy π_ : _S × A →_ [0 _,_ 1] parameterised by **x** . The agent obtains a reward _rt_ +1 _∼R_ ( **s** _t,_ **a** _t,_ **s** _t_ +1) based on the transition **s** _t_ +1 _∼P_ ( **s** _t_ +1 _|_ **s** _t,_ **a** _t_ ). The _action-value_ of the agent’s policy given a state **s** 0 and action **a** 0 is given by _Q_ **x** ( **s** 0 _,_ **a** 0) := E[[�] _[∞] t_ =0 _[γ][t][r][t]_[+1] _[|]_ **[s]**[0] _[,]_ **[ a]**[0] _[, π]_ **[x]**[]][ under discount rate] _γ ∈_ [0 _,_ 1). The corresponding _value_ of policy _π_ **x** is given by _V_ **x** ( **s** 0) := E **a** 0 _∼π_ **x** ( **a** _|_ **s** 0)[ _Q_ **x** ( **s** 0 _,_ **a** 0)].

The agent’s problem is to learn a policy that maximises the value given an expectation over **s** 0, defined either by an initial state distribution in the episodic setting (e.g. Atari, Section 5.2) or the stationary state-visitation distribution under the policy in the non-episodic setting (Section 5.1). Central to RL is the notion of _policy-improvement_ , which takes a current policy _π_ **x** and constructs a new policy _π_ **x** _′_ such that E[ _V_ **x** _′_ ] _≥_ E[ _V_ **x** ]. A common policy-improvement step is arg max **x** _′_ E _a∼π_ **x** _′_ ( _a|s_ )[ _Q_ **x** ( _s, a_ )].

Most works in meta-RL rely on actor-critic algorithms (Sutton et al., 1999). These treat the above policy-improvement step as an optimisation problem and estimate a _policy-gradient_ (Williams & Peng, 1991; Sutton et al., 1999) to optimise **x** . To estimate _V_ **x** , these introduce a _critic v_ **z** that is jointly trained with the policy. The policy is optimised under the current estimate of its value function, while the critic is tracking the value function by minimizing a Temporal-Difference (TD) error. ## 5.1 A NON-STATIONARY-STATIONARYSTATIONARY AND NON-EPISODIC-EPISODICEPISODIC GRID WORLD

We begin with a tabular grid-world with two items to collect. Once an item is collected, it is randomly re-spawned. One item yields a reward of +1 and the other a reward of +1 and the other a reward of and the other a reward of _−_ 1.. The reward is flipped every 100,000 steps. To succeed, a memory-less agent must efficiently re-explore the environment. We study an on-policy actor-critic agent with _ϵ_ PG = _ϵ_ TD = 1.. As baseline, we tune a fixed entropy-rate weight _ϵ_ = _ϵ_ EN.. We compare against agents that meta-learn _ϵ_ online. Figure 2 presents our main findings. Both MG and BMG learn adaptive entropy-rate schedules that outperform the baseline. However, MG fails if _ϵ_ = 0 in the meta-objective, as it becomes overly greedy (Figure 9). MG shows no clear benefit of longer meta-learning horizons, indicating that myopia stems from the objective itself. In contrast, BMG exhibits greater adaptive capacity and is able to utilise greater meta-learning horizons. Too short horizons induce myopia, whereas too long prevent efficient adaptation. For a given horizon, increasing _K_ is uniformly beneficial. Finally, we find that BMG outperforms MG for a given horizon without backpropagating through all updates. For instance, for _K_ = 8, BMG outperforms MG with _K_ = 1 and _L_ = 7. Our ablation studies (Appendix B.2) show that increasing the target bootstrap length counters myopia; however, using the meta-learned update rule for all _L_ steps can derail meta-optimization.

Next, we consider a new form of meta-learning: learning _ε_ -greedy exploration in a _Q_ ( _λ_ )-agent (precise formulation in Appendix B.3). While the _ε_ parameter has a similar effect to entropyregularization, _ε_ is a parameter applied in the behaviour-policy while acting. As it does not feature in the loss function, it is not readily optimized by existing meta-gradient approaches. In contrast, BMG can be implemented by matching the policy derived from a target action-value function, precisely as in the actor-critic case. An implication is that BMG can meta-learn _without_ backpropagating through the update rule. Significantly, this opens up to meta-learning (parts of) the _behaviour policy_ , which is hard to achieve in the MG setup as the behaviour policy is not used in the update rule. Figure 3 shows that meta-learning _ε_ -greedy exploration in this environment significantly outperforms the best fixed _ε_ found by hyper-parameter tuning. As in the actor-critic case, we find that BMG responds positively to longer meta-learning horizons (larger _L_ ); see Appendix B.3, Figure 12 for detailed results.

## 5.2 ATARI

High-performing RL agents tend to rely on distributed learning systems to improve data efficiency (Kapturowski et al., 2018; Espeholt et al., 2018). This presents serious challenges for meta-learning as the policy gradient becomes noisy and volatile due to off-policy estimation (Xu et al., 2018; Zahavy et al., 2020). Theorem 1 suggests that BMG can be particularly effective in this setting under the appropriate distance function. To test these predictions, we adapt the Self-Tuning Actor-Critic (STACX; Zahavy et al., 2020) to meta-learn under BMG on the 57 environments in the Atari Arcade Learning Environment (ALE; Bellemare et al., 2013).

**Protocol** We follow the original IMPALA setup (Espeholt et al., 2018), but we do not downsample or gray-scale inputs. Following the literature, we train for 200 million frames and evaluate agent performance by median Human Normalized Score (HNS) across 3 seeds (Espeholt et al., 2018; Xu et al., 2018; Zahavy et al., 2020).

**STACX** The IMPALA actor-critic agent runs multiple actors asynchronously to generate experience for a centralized learner. The learner uses truncated importance sampling to correct for off-policy data in the actor-critic update, which adjusts _ρ_ and _V_[ˆ] in Eq. 4. The STACX agent (Zahavy et al., 2020) is a state-of-the-art meta-RL agent. It builds on IMPALA in two ways: (1) it introduces auxiliary tasks in the form of additional objectives that differ only in their hyper-parameters; (2) it meta-learns the hyper-parameters of each loss function (main and auxiliary). Meta-parameters are given by **w** = ( _γ[i] , ϵ[i]_ PG _[, ϵ]_ EN _[i][, ϵ]_ TD _[i][, λ][i][, α][i]_[)][1+] _i_ =1 _[n]_[, where] _[ λ]_[ and] _[ α]_[ are hyper-parameters of the importance] weighting mechanism and _n_ = 2 denotes the number of auxiliary tasks. STACX uses the IMPALA objective as the meta-objective with _K_ = 1. See Appendix C for a complete description.

**BMG** We conduct ceteris-paribus comparisons that only alter the meta-objective: agent parameter updates are _identical_ to those in STACX. When _L_ = 1, the target takes a gradient step on the original IMPALA loss, and hence the _only_ difference is the form of the meta-objective; they both use the same data and gradient information. For _L >_ 1, the first _L −_ 1 steps bootstrap from the meta-learned update rule itself. To avoid overfitting, each of the _L −_ 1 steps use separate replay data; this extra data is not used anywhere else. To understand matching functions, we test policy matching and value matching. Policy matching is defined by _µ_ (˜ **x** _,_ **x**[(] _[K]_[)] ( **w** )) = KL ( _π_ **x** ˜ _∥ π_ **x** (1)); we also test a symmetric KL-divergence (KL-S). Value matching is defined by _µ_ (˜ **z** _,_ **z**[(1)] ( **w** )) := E�( _vz_ ˜ _− vz_ (1))[2][�] .

Figure 4 presents our main comparison. BMG with _L_ = 1 and policy-matching (KL) obtains a median HNS of ~500%, compared to ~350% for STACX. Recall that for _L_ = 1, BMG uses the same data to compute agent parameter update, target update, and matching loss; hence this is an apples-to-apples comparison. Using both policy matching and value matching (with 0 _._ 25 weight on the latter) further improves the score to ~520% and outperforms STACX across almost all 57 games, with a few minor exceptions (left panel, Figure 4). These results are obtained _without_ tuning hyper-parameters for BMG. Finally, extending the meta-learning horizon by setting _L_ = 4 and adjusting gradient clipping from _._ 3 to _._ 2 obtains a score of ~610%.

In Figure 5, we turn to ablations. In the left-panel, we deconstruct BMG into STACX (i.e., MG) and compare performances. We find that roughly 45% of the performance gains comes from curvature correction (given by using RMSProp in the target bootstrap). The matching function can further control curvature to obtain performance improvements, accounting for roughly 25%. Finally, increasing _L_ , thereby reducing myopia, accounts for about 30% of the performance improvement. Comparing the cosine similarity between consequtive meta-gradients, we find that BMG improves upon STACX by two orders of magnitude. Detailed ablations in Appendix C.1.

The center panel of Figure 5 provides a deep-dive in the effect of increasing the meta-learning horizon ( _L >_ 1) in Ms Pacman. Performance is uniformly increasing in _L_ , providing further support that BMG can increase the effective meta-horizon without increasing the number of update steps to backpropagate through. A more in-depth analysis Appendix C.3 reveals that _K_ is more sensitive to curvature and the quality of data. However, bootstrapping _only_ from the meta-learner for all _L_ steps can lead to degeneracy (Appendix C.2, Figure 14). In terms of replay (Appendix C.2), while standard MG degrades with more replay, BMG benefits from more replay in the target bootstrap.

The right panel of Figure 5 studies the effect of the matching function. Overall, joint policy and value matching exhibits best performance. In contrast to recent work (Tomar et al., 2020; Hessel et al., 2021), we do not find that reversing the KL-direction is beneficial. Using only value-matching results in worse performance, as it does not optimise for efficient policy improvements. Finally, we conduct detailed analysis of scalability in Appendix C.4. While BMG is 20% slower for _K_ = 1 _, L_ = 1 due to the target bootstrap, it is 200% faster when MG uses _K_ = 4 and BMG uses _K_ = 1 _, L_ = 3.

## 6 MULTI-TASK FEW-SHOT LEARNING

Multi-task meta-learning introduces an expectation over task objectives. BMG is applied by computing task-specific bootstrap targets, with the meta-gradient being the expectation over task-specific matching losses. For a general multi-task formulation, see Appendix D; here we focus on the few-shot classification paradigm. Let _fD_ : _X →_ R denote the negative log-likelihood loss on some data _D_ . A _task_ is defined as a pair of datasets ( _Dτ , D[′] τ_[)][, where] _[ D][τ]_[is a training set and] _[ D][′] τ_[is a validation set.] In the _M_ -shot- _N_ -way setting, each task has _N_ classes and _Dτ_ contains _M_ observations per class.

The goal of this experiment is to study how the BMG objective behaves in the multi-task setting. For this purpose, we focus on the canonical MAML setup (Finn et al., 2017), which meta-learns an initialisation **x**[(0)] _τ_ = **w** for SGD that is shared across a task distribution _p_ ( _τ_ ). **Setup** We use the MiniImagenet benchmark (Vinyals et al., 2016) and study two forms of efficiency: for _data efficiency_ , we compare meta-test performance as function of the number of meta-training batches; for _computational efficiency_ , we compare meta-test performance as a function of training time. To reflect what each method would achieve for a given computational budget, we report meta-test performance for the hyper-parameter configuration with best meta-validation performance. For MG, we tune the meta-learning rate _β ∈{_ 10 _[−]_[3] _,_ 10 _[−]_[4] _}_ , _K ∈{_ 1 _,_ 5 _,_ 10 _}_ , and options to use first-order approximations ((FOMAML; Finn et al., 2017) or (ANIL; Raghu et al., 2020)). For BMG, we tune _β ∈{_ 10 _[−]_[3] _,_ 10 _[−]_[4] _}_ , _K ∈{_ 1 _,_ 5 _}_ , as well as _L ∈{_ 1 _,_ 5 _,_ 10 _}_ , and the direction of the KL.

The left panel of Figure 6 presents results on data efficiency. For few meta-updates, MG and BMG are on par. For 50 000 meta-updates and beyond, BMG achieves strictly superior performance, with the performance delta increasing over meta-updates. The central panel presents results on computational efficiency; we plot the time required to reach a given meta-test performance. This describes the relationship between performance and computational complexity. We find BMG exhibits better scaling properties, reaching the best performance of MG in approximately half the time. Finally, in the right panel, we study the effect of varying _K_ . BMG achieves higher performance for both _K_ = 1 and _K_ = 5. We allow MG to also use _K_ = 10, but this did not yield any significant gains. We conduct an analysis of the impact BMG has on curvature and meta-gradient variance in Appendix D.3. To summarise, we find that BMG significantly improves upon the MG meta-objective, both in terms of data efficiency, computational efficiency, and final performance.

## 7 CONCLUSION

In this paper, we have put forth the notion that efficient meta-learning does not require the metaobjective to be expressed directly in terms of the learner’s objective. Instead, we present an alternative approach that relies on having the meta-learner match a desired target. Here, we bootstrap from the meta-learned update rule itself to produce future targets. While using the meta-learned update rule as the bootstrap allows for an open-ended meta-learning process, some grounding is necessary. As an instance of this approach, we study bootstrapped meta-gradients, which can guarantee performance improvements under appropriate choices of targets and matching functions that can be larger than those of standard meta-gradients. Empirically, we observe substantial improvements on Atari and achieve a new state-of-the-art, while obtaining significant efficiency gains in a multi-task metalearning setting. We explore new possibilities afforded by the target-matching nature of the algorithm and demonstrate that it can learn to explore in an _ϵ_ -greedy _Q_ -learning agent.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a novel and conceptually compelling framework—Bootstrapped Meta-Learning (BMG)—that reframes meta-learning as a target-matching process rather than direct gradient-based optimization of a meta-objective. It demonstrates strong empirical gains on Atari and few-shot learning benchmarks, and introduces a mechanism to extend the effective meta-horizon without full backpropagation. The theoretical analysis provides useful local improvement guarantees, and the exploration of meta-learning ε-greedy exploration is a notable conceptual advance.

Overview issues:

- Theoretical guarantees rely on noiseless, deterministic assumptions that do not reflect real RL or few-shot learning settings: The performance guarantees in Section 4 (e.g., Lemma 1 and Theorem 1) are derived under a noiseless setting with true expectations, which is at odds with the high-variance, off-policy, and stochastic environments used in the empirical evaluations (e.g., Atari with IMPALA). In practice, the meta-gradient estimates are noisy due to replay sampling, importance weighting, and asynchronous actors, yet the analysis does not account for this. The gap between the theoretical setting and empirical reality weakens the paper's claim of establishing 'conditions that guarantee performance improvements.' A more realistic analysis incorporating stochasticity or at least a discussion of how noise affects the bootstrap loop would strengthen the contribution.

- Circularity in bootstrapped target generation risks instability or divergence without explicit regularization or convergence analysis: The core mechanism—using the meta-learner to generate its own training targets—creates a potentially unstable feedback loop. While the paper notes that using the meta-updated parameters for all L steps can 'derail meta-optimization' (Appendix C.2), it does not provide a principled explanation or safeguard against this. The absence of a convergence analysis or even a diagnostic (e.g., meta-gradient norm evolution, target drift) in the experiments leaves open the question of whether BMG is robust or prone to runaway feedback in long runs. Given that meta-learning already suffers from non-stationarity in RL, this self-referential update rule demands stronger justification than empirical success alone.

- Key empirical claims lack statistical significance testing and sufficient baseline comparisons: The Atari results claim a 'new state-of-the-art' based on median Human Normalized Score across 3 seeds, but no statistical significance tests are reported, nor are error bars shown in the main Figure 4 (only standard deviation shading). Given the known high variance in Atari scores, this is a serious omission. Moreover, the comparison to STACX is not fully fair: while the paper claims ceteris paribus comparisons, STACX uses K=1 but no ablation shows STACX with K>1, which would be the true baseline for longer horizons. The omission of comparisons to other recent meta-RL methods like VariBAD or Agent57 (beyond just citing them) further weakens the SOTA claim.

- The role of the matching function is under-analyzed, and its choice appears arbitrary in key experiments: While the paper claims the metric 'can control meta-optimisation,' the selection of matching functions (KL, value matching, etc.) is largely empirical and lacks theoretical grounding in the context of the bootstrapped target. For example, in the Atari experiments, a weighted combination of policy and value matching is used, but no ablation justifies the 0.25 weight on value matching. The claim that the metric controls curvature is plausible but not rigorously linked to the actual training dynamics. Without a clearer principle for choosing or designing μ, the method risks appearing as a collection of effective heuristics rather than a principled framework.

- The multi-task few-shot learning results conflate architectural choices with meta-objective improvements: In Section 6, the comparison between BMG and MAML does not isolate the effect of the meta-objective from other factors. The BMG setup uses a target bootstrapped via SGD on validation data, which effectively gives it access to more supervision than MAML, which only uses training data for adaptation. This design choice—using validation data both for target generation and final evaluation—introduces a data leakage concern. Moreover, the paper does not clarify whether the 'expert' target in BMG is computed with knowledge of true labels, which would make the comparison unfair. The efficiency gains could stem from this architectural advantage rather than the meta-objective itself.

- The claim of 'learning to explore' in ε-greedy Q-learning is overstated and lacks ablation: The paper claims BMG can meta-learn ε-greedy exploration 'without backpropagating through the update rule,' which is indeed a novel capability. However, Figure 3 shows only aggregate performance, with no analysis of the learned ε(t) schedule or comparison to hand-designed annealing. It is unclear whether the meta-learner is actually learning a meaningful exploration strategy or simply optimizing for short-term reward. An ablation showing the evolution of ε or its correlation with uncertainty would be necessary to support the claim. Without this, the result remains suggestive rather than conclusive.

- No ablation isolating horizon extension from curvature effects: The paper attributes part of BMG's performance gain to extending the effective meta-learning horizon, but does not disentangle this from curvature correction effects. In Figure 5, the ablation assigns 30% of gains to increasing L, yet the same change also alters the target bootstrap path and interacts with the matching function. A direct comparison is needed between BMG with L > 1 and a modified MG that unrolls L steps with frozen meta-parameters (i.e., using the same multi-step rollout but without bootstrapped updates) to isolate the effect of horizon extension. Without this, the claim that bootstrapping extends the horizon in a meaningful way—distinct from improved optimization dynamics—remains conflated with other factors like RMSProp in the target computation.

- Missing analysis of bootstrapped target quality in noisy settings: The theoretical analysis assumes noiseless gradients, but the empirical results operate in high-noise regimes (e.g., distributed IMPALA with off-policy data). In such settings, the bootstrapped target is based on noisy parameter updates, which may degrade or distort the learning signal. The paper does not evaluate how faithful the bootstrapped target is to a 'true' optimal update direction—no diagnostics are shown for target variance, alignment with long-horizon improvements, or sensitivity to replay sampling. To support the claim that BMG works by generating useful self-supervised targets, the authors should compute the cosine similarity between the BMG update and a longer unrolled MG (with K >> 1) on a subset of tasks, or measure target consistency across different replay buffers.

- No demonstration of metric control over meta-optimisation dynamics: The paper claims the choice of metric 'can control meta-optimisation', but provides no evidence that μ actively shapes learning beyond performance differences. For instance, it does not show how different μ (e.g., KL vs L2 vs value matching) affect meta-gradient variance, convergence speed, or sensitivity to curvature. A minimal test would be to plot meta-gradient norms and effective learning rates over time for each matching function in the Atari setup. Even more revealing would be to compute the Hessian of the meta-objective with respect to w and compare its spectrum under different μ—this would substantiate the claim that the metric controls curvature. Without such analysis, the role of μ remains descriptive rather than mechanistic.

- No exploration of failure modes or limits of bootstrapping: The paper demonstrates success on several benchmarks but does not probe where or why BMG might fail. For example, in the grid-world, the environment is periodic and relatively simple—does BMG still work if task changes are aperiodic or unobserved? The authors note that using the meta-updated rule for all L steps can 'derail meta-optimization' but do not characterize when this happens. A failure mode analysis could include plotting meta-loss divergence, target drift, or policy collapse under extreme L or poor initialization. At minimum, the paper should test BMG on a deliberately non-stationary task where the meta-learner must adapt to irreversible changes, to show the boundaries of the method’s adaptability.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide a convergence or stability analysis of the bootstrapping loop, or at least include empirical diagnostics (e.g., meta-gradient norm, target drift) to demonstrate robustness.

- Re-evaluate the Atari results with statistical significance testing and include comparisons to STACX with K>1 and other recent SOTA meta-RL methods.

- Clarify the data usage in the few-shot learning experiments to ensure no leakage between target generation and evaluation, and isolate the effect of the meta-objective from architectural choices.

- Add ablation studies for the matching function weights and provide a principled justification for their selection.

- Include analysis of the learned exploration schedule in the ε-greedy experiment to substantiate the claim of meta-learning exploration.

comments.title: Dimensional inconsistency in $G^T = D^T D$ invalidates norm expression
comments.quote: $A$. Let $G^{T}=D^{T}D\in\mathbb{R}^{n_{x}\times n_{z}}$, with $D\coloneqq\left[\frac{\beta}{\beta\mathbf{w}}\,\mathbf{x}^{(K)}(\mathbf{w})\right]^{T}\in\mathbb{R}^{n_{w}\times n
comments.explanation: The claim that $G^T = D^T D \in \mathbb{R}^{n_x \times n_z}$ is dimensionally impossible. If $D \in \mathbb{R}^{n_w \times n_z}$, then $D^T D \in \mathbb{R}^{n_z \times n_z}$, which cannot equal $\mathbb{R}^{n_x \times n_z}$ unless $n_x = n_z$, an assumption not stated. Moreover, $D$ is defined via an ill-formed expression involving $\beta/(\beta \mathbf{w})$, which does not resolve to a Jacobian. This breaks the definition of $\|\cdot\|_{G^T}$ in Lemma 1, which relies on $G^T$ to characterize the meta-gradient norm. The matrix $G^T$ must be redefined with consistent dimensions—likely as $D D^T$ with $D = \partial \mathbf{x}^{(K)}/\partial \mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_x \times n_w}$—to restore validity to the theoretical analysis.

comments.title: Unjustified strict improvement in Corollary 1 due to degenerate condition
comments.quote: Let $\mu=\|\cdot\|_{2}^{2}$ and $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}=\xi_{G}^{r}(\mathbf{x}^{(K)})$. Let $\mathbf{w}^{\prime}$ be given by Eq. 1 and $\tilde{\mathbf{w}}$ be given by Eq. 2. For $\beta$ sufficiently small, $f\big{(}\,\mathbf{x}^{(K)}(\tilde{\mathbf{w}})\big{)}\leq f\big{(}\,\mathbf{x}^{(K)}(\mathbf{w}^{\prime})\big{)}$, strictly if $GG^{T}\neq G^{T}$ and $G^{T}\nabla_{x}f(\mathbf{x}^{(K)})=0$.
comments.explanation: The condition for strict improvement, $G^T \nabla_x f = 0$, implies the meta-gradient vanishes, so MG yields no first-order improvement. In this degenerate case, BMG appears superior only because MG fails. The comparison does not establish BMG's advantage when MG is effective. Furthermore, $GG^T \neq G^T$ is meaningless: $GG^T \in \mathbb{R}^{n_x \times n_x}$ and $G^T \in \mathbb{R}^{n_z \times n_x}$ are incompatible for comparison. The claim should be revised to state that BMG can achieve equal or greater improvement depending on $\xi$, without asserting strict superiority under invalid conditions.

comments.title: Undefined symbol $\mathbf{g}$ in Theorem 1 expansion
comments.quote:
The BMG update satisfies

$f\big{(}\mathbf{x}^{(K)}(\hat{\mathbf{w}})\big{)}-f\big{(}\mathbf{x}^{(K)}(\mathbf{w})\big{)}=\frac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\mu(\tilde{\mathbf{x}},\mathbf{x}^{(K)}-\alpha G^{T}\,\mathbf{g})-\mu(\tilde{\mathbf{x}},\mathbf{x}^{(K)})\right)+o(\beta(\alpha+\beta)).$
comments.explanation: The symbol $\mathbf{g}$ appears without definition. Given context, it must represent $\nabla_x f(\mathbf{x}^{(K)})$, but this is not stated. This omission creates ambiguity in a key theoretical result. Define $\mathbf{g} \coloneqq \nabla_x f(\mathbf{x}^{(K)})$ to eliminate confusion and ensure reproducibility of the analysis.

comments.title: Missing smoothness assumption on $f$ for Taylor expansion
comments.quote: In this analysis, we restrict attention to the noise-less setting (true expectations). In this setting, we ask three questions: (1) what local performance guarantees are provided by MG? (2) What performance guarantees can BMG provide? (3) How do these guarantees relate to each other? To answer these questions, we analyse how the performance around $f(\mathbf{x}^{(K)}(\mathbf{w}))$ changes by updating $\mathbf{w}$ either under standard meta-gradients (Eq. 1) or bootstrapped meta-gradients (Eq. 2).
comments.explanation: Lemma 1 and Theorem 1 rely on Taylor expansions with $O(\beta^2)$ remainder terms, but the paper assumes only that $f$ is a stochastic objective without requiring smoothness. To justify the expansion, $f$ must be twice differentiable with locally bounded Hessian. Without this, the sign of the improvement cannot be guaranteed. Add the assumption that $f$ is twice continuously differentiable in a neighborhood of $\mathbf{x}^{(K)}(\mathbf{w})$ to support the theoretical claims.

comments.title: Insufficient convexity assumption for guaranteed BMG improvement
comments.quote: Next, we turn to BMG, where we assume $\mu$ is differentiable and convex, with $0$ being its minimum.
comments.explanation: Theorem 1 claims infinitely many $\xi$ yield negative improvement, relying on $\mu(\tilde{\mathbf{x}}, \mathbf{x}^{(K)} - \alpha G^T \mathbf{g}) < \mu(\tilde{\mathbf{x}}, \mathbf{x}^{(K)})$. But if $\mu$ is convex but not strictly convex (e.g., $\mu(a,b) = |a_1 - b_1|$), the decrease may not occur even for non-zero displacement. The current assumption does not ensure improvement. Strengthen it to require $\mu$ strictly convex or add that $G^T \mathbf{g}$ lies in a direction of strict decrease.

comments.title: Ambiguous and inconsistent value matching definition
comments.quote: Value matching is defined by  $\mu(\tilde{\mathbf{z}}, \mathbf{z}^{(1)}(\mathbf{w})) \coloneqq \mathbb{E}\big[(v_{\tilde{z}} - v_{z^{(1)}})^2\big]$ .
comments.explanation: The notation is inconsistent: $\tilde{\mathbf{z}}$ and $\mathbf{z}^{(1)}(\mathbf{w})$ are parameters, but $v_{\tilde{z}}$ and $v_{z^{(1)}}$ are not defined as functions of them. The expectation $\mathbb{E}$ lacks a specified measure—over states, trajectories, or data? In contrast, policy matching is clearly defined via KL divergence. Define $v_z$ explicitly (e.g., $v_z(s) = v_{\mathbf{z}}(s)$) and specify the domain of the expectation to ensure reproducibility.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces Bootstrapped Meta-Learning (BML), a novel meta-learning approach that uses bootstrapped targets and distance-based matching to overcome myopia and curvature limitations in standard meta-gradient methods. The method achieves state-of-the-art results on Atari and improves efficiency in few-shot learning. However, several critical issues need addressing regarding theoretical assumptions, empirical validation, and methodological clarity.

The paper presents an innovative approach to meta-learning with strong empirical results on challenging benchmarks. The core idea of using bootstrapped targets and distance matching is compelling and well-motivated. The theoretical analysis provides some performance guarantees but relies on restrictive assumptions. The empirical evaluation is extensive but lacks some critical controls and analyses that would fully support the claims.

Overview issues:

- Unjustified Theoretical Assumptions: The performance guarantees in Theorem 1 rely on a noiseless setting and require that target bootstraps align with local gradient directions. These assumptions are unrealistic for high-dimensional RL problems like Atari where stochastic gradients and non-stationarity are fundamental. The paper provides no evidence that these conditions hold in practice, nor does it analyze how violations affect performance. This creates a significant gap between theoretical claims and empirical validation. The authors should either relax these assumptions or demonstrate empirically that the conditions are approximately satisfied in their experimental settings.

- Insufficient Analysis of Bootstrap Bias and Variance: The paper claims that bootstrapping extends the effective meta-learning horizon without backpropagation through all updates, but provides no theoretical or empirical analysis of the bias-variance trade-off introduced by the bootstrap targets. Section 6 mentions that targets should co-align with gradient direction but does not quantify how misalignment affects optimization. In the Atari experiments, using the meta-learned update rule for all L bootstrap steps can lead to degeneracy (Appendix C.2), suggesting potential instability issues. The authors need to characterize when and why bootstrapping fails, and provide guidelines for choosing L and target strategies to avoid instability.

- Missing Ablation on Matching Function Design: While the paper experiments with different matching functions (L2, KL, value matching), it lacks systematic analysis of how the choice of matching function interacts with task structure and bootstrap strategy. The ablation in Figure 5 shows performance differences but provides no insight into why certain combinations work better. The theoretical analysis treats μ as a generic convex function, but the empirical results suggest that the specific choice matters significantly. The authors should analyze how matching function properties affect optimization dynamics and provide design principles beyond empirical trial-and-error.

- Inadequate Baseline Comparisons in RL Experiments: The Atari comparisons are primarily against STACX, which the authors reimplement. While this provides a controlled comparison, it does not establish whether BMG improves over other strong meta-RL baselines like RL^2, PEARL, or Meta-World methods. The paper claims state-of-the-art performance but the comparison set is limited. Additionally, the computational efficiency claims (200% faster) need verification against more baselines beyond just MG with different K values. The authors should include comparisons with a broader set of meta-RL methods and provide more comprehensive computational analysis.

- Limited Robustness Analysis for Non-Stationary Settings: The grid-world experiment demonstrates adaptation to non-stationarity, but the paper lacks systematic analysis of BMG's robustness to different types of non-stationarity and non-episodic conditions. The assumption red flags specifically mention concern about robustness to non-stationarity, yet the paper only tests one simple environment. Real-world RL problems exhibit various forms of non-stationarity (reward shifts, dynamics changes) that are not explored. The authors should test BMG under diverse non-stationary scenarios and analyze failure modes to support claims about robustness.

- Unclear Scalability Analysis for High-Dimensional Problems: The paper claims scalability to high-dimensional problems like Atari and shows computational efficiency gains, but does not analyze how BMG scales with problem dimensionality, network size, or task complexity. The 20% slowdown for K=1, L=1 suggests non-trivial overhead, yet there's no analysis of how this scales. The few-shot learning experiments use MiniImagenet but don't test on larger datasets or more complex architectures. The authors need to provide systematic scalability analysis and discuss computational trade-offs more thoroughly.

- Missing concrete worked example: The paper would benefit from a fully worked numerical example demonstrating BMG on a simple optimization problem. While Section 4 provides theoretical guarantees, readers would gain intuition from seeing the algorithm applied step-by-step to a concrete quadratic function or simple neural network. This should include computing the target bootstrap, matching function, and resulting meta-gradient update, showing how BMG differs from standard meta-gradients in this controlled setting. Such an example would bridge the gap between the abstract theory and the complex empirical results.

- No demonstration of non-vacuous theoretical result: Theorem 1 guarantees performance improvements under certain conditions, but the paper never demonstrates these conditions are satisfied in practice. A concrete example is needed showing that for a specific problem and choice of target bootstrap, the BMG update indeed yields a larger performance improvement than MG as predicted by Corollary 1. This could be shown on a simple convex function where the gradient norms and Jacobians can be computed analytically, verifying the theoretical improvement bound holds numerically. Without this, the theoretical results remain abstract guarantees with no empirical grounding.

- Underdeveloped practical implementation guidance: The paper claims BMG enables new forms of meta-learning like meta-learning ε-greedy exploration, but provides insufficient implementation details for practitioners. Section 5.1 mentions this can be done 'without backpropagating through the update rule' but doesn't specify how to compute the matching function when the ε parameter doesn't appear in the loss. A complete description of the algorithmic steps needed to meta-behavior policies would strengthen the paper's claims about enabling new meta-learning possibilities. This should include pseudocode showing how to handle parameters that affect behavior but not the loss function.

- Missing recovery of standard meta-gradient as special case: While Section 4 states that standard meta-gradient is a special case of BMG, the paper never explicitly verifies this recovery in practice. A concrete demonstration showing that with specific choices of matching function (μ) and target bootstrap (ξ), the BMG update numerically equals the MG update would validate this claim. This could be shown on a simple problem where both methods are implemented and their updates compared. Such verification is important for establishing that BMG truly generalizes standard meta-gradients rather than being an unrelated algorithm.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide theoretical analysis of bias-variance trade-off in bootstrapped targets and conditions for stability

- Include systematic ablation studies on matching function design and its interaction with task structure

- Add comparisons with broader set of meta-RL baselines beyond STACX in Atari experiments

- Conduct robustness analysis across diverse non-stationary and non-episodic environments

- Provide comprehensive scalability analysis with respect to problem dimensionality and computational budget

comments.title: Matrix dimension mismatch in D definition
comments.quote: Let $G^{T}=D^{T}D\in\mathbb{R}^{n_{x}\times n_{z}}$, with $D\coloneqq\left[\frac{\beta}{\beta\mathbf{w}}\,\mathbf{x}^{(K)}(\mathbf{w})\right]^{T}\in\mathbb{R}^{n_{w}\times n_{z}}$.
comments.explanation: The dimension analysis reveals an inconsistency. If $D \in \mathbb{R}^{n_{w} \times n_{z}}$, then $D^{T}D \in \mathbb{R}^{n_{z} \times n_{z}}$, which contradicts the stated dimension $\mathbb{R}^{n_{x} \times n_{z}}$ for $G^{T}$. Additionally, the expression $\frac{\beta}{\beta\mathbf{w}}$ is mathematically unclear as it appears to divide a scalar by a vector. The notation $\left[\frac{\beta}{\beta\mathbf{w}}\,\mathbf{x}^{(K)}(\mathbf{w})\right]^{T}$ is also malformed. Rewrite the definition of D to ensure consistent dimensions and clarify the mathematical expression.

comments.title: Contradiction in Corollary 1 strictness condition
comments.quote: For $\beta$ sufficiently small, $f\big{(}\,\mathbf{x}^{(K)}(\tilde{\mathbf{w}})\big{)}\leq f\big{(}\,\mathbf{x}^{(K)}(\mathbf{w}^{\prime})\big{)}$, strictly if $GG^{T}\neq G^{T}$ and $G^{T}\nabla_{x}f(\mathbf{x}^{(K)})=0$.

comments.title: Entropy objective sign error
comments.quote: $\ell_{\text{EN}}(\mathbf{x})$ $=\sum_{t\in\tau}\sum_{\mathbf{a}\in\mathcal{A}}\pi_{\mathbf{x}}(\mathbf{a}\mid\mathbf{s}_{t})\log\pi_{\mathbf{x}}(\mathbf{a}\mid\mathbf{s}_{t}),\qquad\ell_{\text{TD}}(\mathbf{z})=\frac{1}{2}\sum_{t\in\tau}\Big{(}G_{t}^{(n)}-v_{\mathbf{z}}(\mathbf{s}_{t})\Big{)}^{2},$ (4)
comments.explanation: The entropy objective is incorrectly signed. For a probability distribution $\pi_{\mathbf{x}}(\mathbf{a}|\mathbf{s}_t) \in [0,1]$, we have $\log \pi_{\mathbf{x}}(\mathbf{a}|\mathbf{s}_t) \leq 0$, making $\sum_{\mathbf{a}} \pi_{\mathbf{x}}(\mathbf{a}|\mathbf{s}_t) \log \pi_{\mathbf{x}}(\mathbf{a}|\mathbf{s}_t) \leq 0$. This is the negative entropy. Since the objective $f(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{z})$ is minimized (as indicated by the TD error term being squared), the current formulation minimizes negative entropy, which discourages exploration. The standard entropy regularization should maximize entropy, requiring $\ell_{\text{EN}}(\mathbf{x}) = -\sum_{t\in\tau}\sum_{\mathbf{a}\in\mathcal{A}}\pi_{\mathbf{x}}(\mathbf{a}\mid\mathbf{s}_{t})\log\pi_{\mathbf{x}}(\mathbf{a}\mid\mathbf{s}_{t})$.

comments.title: Undefined importance weight ρ_t
comments.quote: where $\rho_{t}$ denotes an importance weight and $G_{t}^{(n)}$ denotes an $n$-step bootstrap target.
comments.explanation: The text introduces $\rho_t$ as an importance weight but never defines its form or relationship to the behavior and target policies. In off-policy learning, $\rho_t$ typically equals $\frac{\pi_{\mathbf{x}}(\mathbf{a}_t|\mathbf{s}_t)}{\mu(\mathbf{a}_t|\mathbf{s}_t)}$ where $\mu$ is the behavior policy, but this is not stated. Without this definition, readers cannot verify the correctness of the policy gradient objective or understand how sampling bias is corrected. Add the definition $\rho_t = \frac{\pi_{\mathbf{x}}(\mathbf{a}_t|\mathbf{s}_t)}{\mu(\mathbf{a}_t|\mathbf{s}_t)}$ after the sentence introducing $\rho_t$ to clarify how importance sampling corrects for the distribution mismatch.

comments.title: Undefined bootstrap target G_t^(n)
comments.quote: where $\rho_{t}$ denotes an importance weight and $G_{t}^{(n)}$ denotes an $n$-step bootstrap target.
comments.explanation: The bootstrap target $G_t^{(n)}$ is referenced but only partially defined later for the on-policy case. The text states 'its form depends on the algorithm' but never provides the general off-policy formulation. In off-policy settings, $G_t^{(n)}$ typically includes importance-weighted returns: $G_t^{(n)} = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} (\prod_{j=0}^{i-1} \gamma \rho_{t+j}) \gamma^i r_{t+i+1} + (\prod_{j=0}^{n-1} \gamma \rho_{t+j}) \gamma^n v_{\bar{\mathbf{z}}}(\mathbf{s}_{t+n})$. Without this definition, the off-policy objective is incomplete. Add the general form of $G_t^{(n)}$ for the off-policy setting after mentioning that its form depends on the algorithm.

comments.title: Meta-learning ε-greedy mechanism unclear
comments.quote: In contrast, BMG can be implemented by matching the policy derived from a target action-value function, precisely as in the actor-critic case.
comments.explanation: The mechanism for meta-learning ε-greedy exploration via policy matching is not mathematically clear. For ε-greedy policies, the behavior policy π_b(a|s) = ε/|A| + (1-ε)·argmax_a Q(s,a) contains a discrete random component that doesn't directly appear in the Q-function. The KL-divergence KL(π_\hat{x} || π_{x^{(K)}}) between policies requires both policies to be properly defined. The text should explain: (1) how the target policy π_\hat{x} is constructed from the target Q-function, (2) how the gradient ∂KL/∂ε is computed when ε only affects the random action selection probability, and (3) why this approach works for BMG but not MG. Add: 'For ε-greedy, we construct π_\hat{x}(a|s) = ε/|A| + (1-ε)·1[a = argmax_a \hat{Q}(s,a)], allowing ∂KL/∂ε to be computed through the discrete action probabilities.'

comments.title: Undefined KL direction parameter
comments.quote: For BMG, we tune  $\beta \in \{10^{-3}, 10^{-4}\}$ ,  $K \in \{1, 5\}$ , as well as  $L \in \{1, 5, 10\}$ , and the direction of the KL.
comments.explanation: The text mentions tuning 'the direction of the KL' but never defines what this means or what the possible directions are. This appears to be a critical hyperparameter that affects the BMG objective, but readers cannot understand or reproduce the experiments without knowing what KL direction options were considered. The paper should specify whether this refers to forward KL (KL(p||q)) vs reverse KL (KL(q||p)), or some other directional choice, and list the specific options that were tuned.

comments.title: Inconsistent hyperparameter search space
comments.quote: For MG, we tune the meta-learning rate  $\beta \in \{10^{-3}, 10^{-4}\}$ ,  $K \in \{1, 5, 10\}$ , and options to use first-order approximations ((FOMAML; Finn et al., 2017) or (ANIL; Raghu et al., 2020)). For BMG, we tune  $\beta \in \{10^{-3}, 10^{-4}\}$ ,  $K \in \{1, 5\}$ , as well as  $L \in \{1, 5, 10\}$ , and the direction
comments.explanation: The hyperparameter search spaces are inconsistent between MG and BMG in ways that could bias the comparison. MG is allowed to use $K=10$ while BMG is limited to $K \in \{1, 5\}$. Since the right panel shows BMG outperforming MG for both $K=1$ and $K=5$, but MG shows no improvement when allowed $K=10$, this creates an unfair comparison. If BMG were also allowed $K=10$, the relative performance might change. The paper should either use identical search spaces or justify why BMG cannot use $K=10$.

comments.title: Missing definition of validation performance selection
comments.quote: To reflect what each method would achieve for a given computational budget, we report meta-test performance for the hyper-parameter configuration with best meta-validation performance.
comments.explanation: The paper states it reports performance for the configuration with 'best meta-validation performance' but never defines the meta-validation procedure. Readers need to know: (1) what validation data was used, (2) how many validation tasks were sampled, (3) what metric was used to determine 'best' performance, and (4) whether this validation was performed during training or as a separate sweep. Without these details, the claim of fair comparison based on validation performance cannot be verified.

comments.title: Undefined matching function μ
comments.quote: The BMG objective is the expected distance,  $\mathbb{E}[\mu (\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{\tau},\mathbf{x}_{\tau}^{(K)})]$ .
comments.explanation: The paper introduces the BMG objective using a matching function μ but never defines what μ is. While the text later mentions 'The KL-divergence as matching function has an interesting connection to MG,' it doesn't explicitly state that μ is KL divergence or provide its formal definition. This makes the mathematical formulation incomplete. Add 'where μ is a distance function between parameters, which we implement as KL divergence' after the quoted sentence.

comments.title: Undefined temperature parameter
comments.quote: Raising the temperature in the target can allow BMG to transfer more information (Hinton & Plaut, 1987).
comments.explanation: The text mentions 'Raising the temperature in the target' but never introduces or defines a temperature parameter in the BMG formulation. This appears to be an important hyperparameter for the KL divergence implementation, but it's missing from the hyperparameter tuning list and the mathematical description. Add a definition of the temperature parameter T and include it in the hyperparameter search space.
